name: GR 190: Altos Valles Ibéricos Loop
name_original: Puerto de Peña Hincada, GR-93, Peñaloscintos – ruta circular
description: Hike the GR 190: Altos Valles Ibéricos Loop, a difficult 16.9-mile trail through La Rioja's diverse Iberian mountain ranges.
region_intro: Hiking around Villoslada De Cameros offers access to the Sierra de Cameros mountains and the Sierra Cebollera Natural Park in La Rioja. The region features rounded mountain terrain, dense forests of Scots pine, beech, and Pyrenean oak, and clearings that transition into high mountain pastures. Water is a prominent element, with the Iregua and Leza rivers carving canyons and numerous waterfalls present throughout the landscape. Best hiking trails around Villoslada De Cameros The most popular hiking route is Old Lomos de Orios Trail, a 6.1 miles (9.8 km) trail that takes 3 hours 15 minutes to complete, leading through dense forests to the historic Ermita de Lomos de Orios. Another top favourite among local hikers is GR 190: Altos Valles Ibéricos Loop, a difficult 16.9 miles (27.1 km) path. This route traverses diverse Iberian mountain ranges, offering varied terrain and sustained ascents. Local hikers also love the Puente Ra – Cascada Mayor de Puente Ra loop from Lomos de Orios, a 3.2 miles (5.1 km) trail leading through lush riverside woodlands to picturesque waterfalls, often completed in about 1 hour 40 minutes. Hiking around Villoslada De Cameros is defined by dense forests, mountain terrain, and numerous waterfalls, offering routes for varying ability levels. The routes in Villoslada De Cameros are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.5 stars from over 100 reviews. More than 1500 hikers have used komoot to explore Villoslada De Cameros's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many hiking trails are available around Villoslada de Cameros?
A: There are over 40 hiking trails around Villoslada de Cameros, offering a wide range of options for different skill levels. This includes 5 easy routes, 26 moderate routes, and 10 difficult routes, ensuring there's something for everyone.

Q: What is the best time of year for hiking in Villoslada de Cameros?
A: The autumn months are particularly spectacular for hiking in Villoslada de Cameros, as the forests of Scots pine, beech, and Pyrenean oak transform into a vibrant mosaic of yellows, ochres, and reds. Spring and early summer also offer pleasant conditions with lush greenery and flowing waterfalls.

Q: Are there any hikes that feature waterfalls near Villoslada de Cameros?
A: Yes, the region is known for its beautiful waterfalls. A popular option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38162153" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puente Ra – Cascada Mayor de Puente Ra loop from Lomos de Orios&lt;/a&gt;, which leads through lush riverside woodlands to picturesque waterfalls like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1253838" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cascada Mayor de Puente Ra&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Can I find any glacial lagoons or lakes on hikes in the area?
A: Yes, the nearby Urbión Mountains feature impressive glacial lagoons. You can explore areas with natural lakes such as &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3844856" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Laguna Larga&lt;/a&gt;, &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5647099" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Laguna Verde&lt;/a&gt;, and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2089062" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cebollera Lagoon&lt;/a&gt;, which offer beautiful natural settings for exploration.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on hikes in Sierra Cebollera Natural Park?
A: Hikes in Sierra Cebollera Natural Park feature diverse terrain, including rounded mountain landscapes, dense forests of Scots pine, beech, and Pyrenean oak, and clearings that transition into high mountain pastures. You'll also encounter mountain streams, dramatic canyons like the Leza River, and areas with significant elevation changes.

Q: Are there easy hiking trails suitable for beginners or families?
A: Yes, there are several easy trails perfect for beginners or families. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/17543810" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Laguna de La Nava loop from Villoslada de Cameros&lt;/a&gt; is an easy 5.65 km route. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38162153" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puente Ra – Cascada Mayor de Puente Ra loop&lt;/a&gt; is another accessible option, offering beautiful scenery without significant difficulty.

Q: Are dogs allowed on the hiking trails in Sierra Cebollera Natural Park?
A: Generally, dogs are permitted on trails within Sierra Cebollera Natural Park, but it's always recommended to keep them on a leash to protect local wildlife and ensure the safety of other hikers. Please check specific park regulations or signage at trailheads for any particular restrictions.

Q: What historical or cultural landmarks can I see while hiking?
A: Many trails offer glimpses into the region's history. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e989483463/old-lomos-de-orios-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Old Lomos de Orios Trail&lt;/a&gt;, for instance, leads through dense forests to the historic Ermita de Lomos de Orios, a baroque hermitage situated at 1465 meters. The charming historic center of Villoslada de Cameros itself, with its cobbled streets and medieval bridge, is also worth exploring.

Q: Is parking available at trailheads in Villoslada de Cameros?
A: Villoslada de Cameros serves as a primary gateway to the Sierra Cebollera Natural Park, and many trailheads, especially those starting directly from the town or nearby villages, typically offer designated parking areas. It's advisable to arrive early during peak seasons to secure a spot.

Q: What do other hikers say about the trails in Villoslada de Cameros?
A: The trails in Villoslada de Cameros are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.5 stars from over 100 reviews. Hikers frequently praise the region's dense forests, the beauty of the waterfalls, and the varied terrain that offers routes for all ability levels.

Q: Are there any long-distance or challenging hikes in the area?
A: Yes, for experienced hikers seeking a challenge, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e989599329/gr-190-altos-valles-ibericos-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;GR 190: Altos Valles Ibéricos Loop&lt;/a&gt; is a difficult 27.1 km trail that traverses diverse Iberian mountain ranges, offering varied terrain and sustained ascents. Another challenging option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/6952994" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cascada Mayor de Puente Ra – Puente Ra loop&lt;/a&gt;, which is a 20.3 km difficult route.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain will I encounter on the GR 190: Altos Valles Ibéricos Loop?
faqs.answer: The GR 190 offers a diverse range of landscapes. You'll hike through high mountain wetlands, dense forests, and expansive pastures. The trail follows ancient roads, bridle paths, hidden trails, and ancestral cattle tracks. Expect varied terrain, from humid high mountain areas in the Sierra de La Demanda to more open pastures in the Las Viniegras region.

faqs.question: How is the GR 190 typically divided into stages?
faqs.answer: The official route is approximately 64 km (40 miles) and is commonly divided into three main stages. A common interpretation also covers 75 km (47 miles) across four stages. For example, Stage 1 runs from Ezcaray to Valvanera (around 23.3 km), Stage 2 from Valvanera to Viniegra de Abajo (around 16.3 km), and Stage 3 from Viniegra de Abajo to Villoslada de Cameros (around 24.5 km). An optional fourth stage can extend to Ortigosa de Cameros.

faqs.question: What are some notable landmarks or features along the route?
faqs.answer: The trail takes you through the impressive high Iberian valleys, showcasing traditional mountain architecture and the unique culture of local shepherds. You'll pass by the Peña Hincada Pass, cross the Ortigosa Viaduct, and visit the charming settlement of Peñaloscintos. The River Urbión, originating in the Picos de Urbión Mountains, also crosses through Viniegra, a village along the route.

faqs.question: When is the best time of year to hike the Altos Valles Ibéricos Loop?
faqs.answer: The best seasons for hiking the GR 190 are spring, summer, and autumn. Winter often brings significant snow, especially in the higher mountain areas, making some sections challenging or impassable. Spring and autumn offer pleasant temperatures and beautiful scenery, while summer is ideal for longer daylight hours.

faqs.question: Are there places to stay or resupply along the GR 190?
faqs.answer: Yes, several villages along the route provide accommodation and services where you can resupply. Additionally, there are free-to-use mountain refuges available, though these typically lack services. Planning your stages around these villages is recommended for comfort and convenience.

faqs.question: Is the GR 190 suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: The GR 190 is generally classified with a difficulty ranging from 'moderate' to 'notable' (red difficulty category). Some sections are quite demanding due to their length or significant elevation gains. A good level of mountain fitness is recommended, especially as the first three stages can be quite long. It's not ideal for absolute beginners without prior hiking experience.

faqs.question: Can I bring my dog on the GR 190 trail?
faqs.answer: While the GR 190 traverses natural areas, including parts of the Sierra de Cebollera Natural Park, specific regulations for dogs can vary. It's generally advisable to keep dogs on a leash to protect wildlife and ensure a pleasant experience for all hikers. Always check local signage and park rules for the most current information regarding pets.

faqs.question: Do I need any permits or pay entrance fees for the GR 190?
faqs.answer: No specific permits or entrance fees are generally required to hike the GR 190 itself. However, parts of the trail pass through the Sierra de Cebollera Natural Park. While there are no entrance fees for the park, it's always good practice to be aware of and respect any specific regulations regarding camping, fires, or protected areas within the park boundaries.

faqs.question: Where can I find parking and trailhead access for the GR 190?
faqs.answer: The GR 190 is a multi-stage trail, and various villages serve as starting or ending points for stages. For example, Ezcaray is a common starting point, and you can typically find parking within or near these villages. It's advisable to research specific parking options for your chosen trailhead (e.g., Ezcaray, Valvanera, Viniegra de Abajo, Villoslada de Cameros, Ortigosa de Cameros) before you begin your hike.

faqs.question: Does the GR 190 intersect with other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the GR 190 shares sections with other official trails. Notably, it covers a significant portion of the 'Altos Valles Ibéricos' trail (83%) and also intersects with parts of the 'Ruta romana del Iregua tramo 3' (40%) and 'Sierras Riojanas' (19%). You might also briefly encounter sections of the 'GR-93/Vía Romana del Iregua' and other local paths.

faqs.question: What should I pack for a multi-day hike on the GR 190?
faqs.answer: Given the varied terrain and potential for changing weather, a multi-day packing list should include sturdy hiking boots, layered clothing (including waterproof and windproof outer layers), a first-aid kit, navigation tools (map, compass, or GPS), sufficient water and snacks, sun protection, and appropriate gear for overnight stays if you plan to use refuges or accommodation. Don't forget a power bank for your devices, as charging points might be limited.
